preFix.title: Long-exhale breath for perimenopause anxiety
preFix.subtitle: A five-minute breathwork practice for the new anxiety that arrives with hormonal transition
preFix.excerpt: A five-minute breathwork practice for the racing heart, shallow breathing, and body-level dread that perimenopause anxiety produces. The extended exhale activates the parasympathetic nervous system directly. Useful when the anxiety has a physical, not psychological, quality.
preFix.sourceNotes: Extended exhale breathing is a well-documented autonomic nervous system regulation technique, drawing on the vagal brake mechanism. No single source. Public-domain breathwork lineage, adapted for perimenopause context by homemade.education.

content.text: A five-minute breathwork practice for the body-level anxiety that perimenopause produces. When anxiety is physical first, the extended exhale calms the body faster than thinking does. Pounding heart. Shallow breath. Dread before thought. The exhale works on all of these.
Sit or lie down. Hands loose in your lap or on your belly. Eyes closed or soft-focused on a fixed point.
Breathe in through the nose for a count of four.
Breathe out slowly through the mouth for a count of six to eight. The exhale is longer than the inhale. This is the active part.
Repeat six times at your own pace. If four-in feels too long, use a three count. The ratio matters more than the number.
After six rounds, return to your normal breathing. Sit for a moment before you stand or continue.

Extended exhale breathing draws on the vagal brake. A long, slow exhale activates the body's calming system. This is public-domain physiology used in many breathwork traditions. The perimenopause framing is by homemade.education.
